answer: AKS initiates auto-upgrades for unsupported clusters when a cluster in an n-3 version (where n is the latest supported AKS minor version that's generally available) is about to drop to n-4; AKS automatically upgrades the cluster to n-2 to remain in an AKS support policy.

When using `--os-sku Ubuntu`, AKS defaults to Ubuntu 22.04 in Kubernetes versions 1.25-1.34 and Ubuntu 24.04 in Kubernetes versions 1.35+. In some scenarios, like FIPS-enabled node pools, the default OS version might differ.

When specifying a custom resource group name for the AKS node resource group, you can only do so when you create the cluster. You can't specify an existing resource group, a different subscription, or change the node resource group name after creation. You also can't specify names for the managed resources within the node resource group or modify/delete Azure-created tags of managed resources within the node resource group.

After the cluster is created, the following actions are explicitly unsupported:
- Specifying an existing resource group for the node resource group
- Specifying a different subscription for the node resource group
- Changing the node resource group name
- Specifying names for managed resources within the node resource group
- Modifying or deleting Azure-created tags of managed resources within the node resource group
- Modifying any resource under the node resource group (this can cause cluster operation failures)
AKS supports four main Linux operating systems, and the default OS is Ubuntu Linux when `--os-type Linux` is specified during node pool creation.

Modifying or deleting Azure-created tags on resources under the node resource group in the AKS cluster is an unsupported action, which breaks the service-level objective (SLO) and can cause unexpected scaling and upgrading errors.
